title: 🎙️ Cariboo Local Affairs
description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Cariboo Voices & Local News' — stories FROM and ABOUT the Cariboo-Chilcotin region and its communities. Score 80-100 for articles directly covering: news and events in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, Horsefly, Likely, Anahim Lake, Alexis Creek, or the Cariboo Regional District; local government and civic decisions (City of Williams Lake, City of Quesnel, CRD, District of 100 Mile House); local health services and emergency closures; local economy, businesses, and community programs; Cariboo arts, culture, and community events; AND digital equity, rural internet access (fiber, fixed wireless, Starlink), and connectivity serving these specific communities. Articles from local Cariboo news sources (mycariboonow.com, wltribune.com, quesnelobserver.com, 100milefreepress.net, cfjctoday.com) covering Cariboo community life should score 65-85. Score 50-79 for: BC provincial news directly affecting Cariboo communities, rural Canadian community stories closely paralleling Cariboo life, digital equity and rural internet broadly. Score 20-49 for: general BC news that may affect rural areas, broader rural Canada topics without specific Cariboo tie-in. Score 0-19 for: national/international news without Cariboo relevance, pure tech or entertainment with no community angle. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): international geopolitical and war news with no Cariboo tie-in; science discoveries without a local community connection; consumer tech reviews and gadget guides; wildlife and conservation stories not involving Cariboo communities or First Nations; BC provincial news without direct Cariboo-region relevance. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Heavy marijuana smoking may increase cancer risk, researchers warn
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Heavy marijuana smoking may raise the risk of lung cancer and several head and neck cancers, according to growing research, but many important questions remain unanswered. Scientists are still trying to determine how much marijuana use is enough to significantly increase cancer risk. Edibles have not been linked to lung cancer so far, while the long-term effects of vaping marijuana and secondhand marijuana smoke are still being investigated.
items.summary: Heavy marijuana smoking may raise the risk of lung cancer and several head and neck cancers, according to growing research, but many important questions remain unanswered. Scientists are still trying to determine how much marijuana use is enough to significantly increase cancer risk. Edibles have
items._excerpt: Heavy marijuana smoking may raise the risk of lung cancer and several head and neck cancers, according to growing research, but many important questions remain unanswered. Edibles have not been linked to lung cancer so far, while the long-term effects of vaping marijuana and secondhand marijuana smoke are still being investigated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Experimental drug reverses severe fatty liver disease by repairing the gut
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

An experimental drug called DT-109 reversed severe fatty liver disease in animal studies by repairing the gut and preventing harmful toxins from damaging the liver. The discovery could open the door to a new class of treatments for MASH and potentially other diseases tied to gut health.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Los Angeles police pause Flock's surveillance of everyday life
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Los Angeles police are ending their three-year agreement with Flock Safety after privacy and civil liberties concerns, which is what happens when "neighborhood safety camera" starts sounding a lot like "citywide photographic dragnet."

KABC reports that LAPD will allow its agreement with Flock Safety to expire Saturday, ending a three-year relationship with the company. — Read the rest

The post Los Angeles police pause Flock's surveillance of everyday life appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists warn invasive Asian mantises are threatening Europe's wildlife
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Two striking Asian praying mantis species that have rapidly spread across Europe have now been officially classified as invasive, raising new concerns about their impact on native wildlife. Boosted by climate change and urban environments, these fast-breeding predators are expanding northward, where they prey on native insects, pollinators, and even small vertebrates while also reducing native mantis populations through deadly mating interactions.
items.summary: Two striking Asian praying mantis species that have rapidly spread across Europe have now been officially classified as invasive, raising new concerns about their impact on native wildlife. Boosted by climate change and urban environments, these fast-breeding predators are expanding northward,

items.content_html:

If you've ever had to work out when to start and end your daily intermittent fasting period for the best results, this new study may help. Researchers have found that as long as your fast happens, it's effective for long-term weight loss regardless of the time you clock on and off.

items.title: [Boing Boing] The British government has kept a cat on the payroll since 1929
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

On June 3, 1929, a Treasury official named A.E. Banham authorized the Office Keeper at 10 Downing Street "to spend 1d a day from petty cash towards the maintenance of an efficient cat." The job remains to this day. Britain's official resident cat now carries the title Chief Mouser to the Cabinet Office, and the current officeholder, Larry — a Battersea rescue picked out by David Cameron's family in 2011 after TV cameras caught rats running across the front steps of Number 10 — is the first cat to hold it officially. — Read the rest

The post The British government has kept a cat on the payroll since 1929 appeared first on Boing Boing.
items.summary: On June 3, 1929, a Treasury official named A.E. Banham authorized the Office Keeper at 10 Downing Street "to spend 1d a day from petty cash towards the maintenance of an efficient cat." The job remains to this day. Britain's official resident cat now carries the title Chief Mouser to the Cabinet
items._excerpt: On June 3, 1929, a Treasury official named A.E. Banham authorized the Office Keeper at 10 Downing Street "to spend 1d a day from petty cash towards the maintenance of an efficient cat." The job remains to this day. Britain's official resident cat now carries the title Chief Mouser to the Cabinet Office, and the current officeholder, Larry — a Battersea rescue picked out by David Cameron's family in 2011 after TV cameras caught rats running across the front steps of Number 10 — is the first cat to hold it officially. — Read the rest The post The British government has kept a cat on the payroll

items.title: [MacRumors] Apple Reportedly Slashes iPhone 17 Demand Forecast Amid Rising Costs
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Apple has significantly lowered its demand expectations for the standard iPhone 17 because of rising hardware costs, a Chinese leaker claimed today.


According to the account "Fixed Focus Digital" on Weibo, some production lines for the standard iPhone 17 this week shifted from an earlier 15 percent reduction to plans to suspend roughly one-third of their capacity. The leaker also claimed Apple has made a "very serious" internal assessment of the impact that higher hardware costs could have on demand.

The claims can't be independently verified, plus the post doesn't say whether the production adjustments apply to overall iPhone 17 output or only to certain manufacturing lines, so caution is advised.

Higher component costs are impacting the electronics industry worldwide due to intense demand from AI companies intent on maximizing data center buildout, and Apple is far from immune.

The doubling cost of memory and storage chips over the last year recently led Apple to increase prices across multiple product lines, which CEO Tim Cook called "unavoidable." So far, iPhones have been spared the hikes, but Apple is widely expected to use the debut of iPhone 18 Pro models in September as a chance to increase the prices of its entire smartphone lineup.

Apple is expected to unveil a next-generation iPhone 18 model next spring alongside a new iPhone 18e and iPhone Air 2, as part of a new split-launch strategy.
Related Roundup: iPhone 17
Tag: Fixed Focus Digital
Buyer's Guide: iPhone 17 (Neutral)
Related Forum: iPhone

This article, "Apple Reportedly Slashes iPhone 17 Demand Forecast Amid Rising Costs" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums
